You are migrating ONE repository to NuGet Central Package Management (CPM) as part of a sweep across the `data-services` GitLab group on `gitlab.rivagecapital.com`.

## Your repo
- Path: `data-services/coremont.common`
- Project id: `1143`
- Default branch: `main`
- Current CPM status: `none` (no Directory.Packages.props)

## Read your task brief first — it is your requirements with exact values:
`C:\Code\dterziev\CoreClaw\.superpowers\sdd\task-3-brief.md`

## Required skills
- Use the **convert-to-cpm** skill for the actual conversion (it captures a baseline build, generates Directory.Packages.props, strips versions from csproj, and compares binlogs). Invoke it via the Skill tool.
- Use the **glab** skill for creating the issue and MR.

## Environment
- OS: Windows; you have a Bash tool (Git Bash, POSIX sh) AND PowerShell. `glab`, `git`, `dotnet`, `jq` are available and `glab` is authenticated.
- Do ALL repo work inside the scratchpad — clone there, do not touch the CoreClaw repo working copy:
  `C:\Users\bhirani\AppData\Local\Temp\3\claude\c--Code-dterziev-CoreClaw\e2f6752b-555b-4db9-ab95-752506132736\scratchpad`
- Clone command: `glab repo clone data-services/coremont.common` (clones into a subdir of the scratchpad; cd into it).

## Procedure (full detail in the brief)
1. Clone into the scratchpad, create branch `chore/adopt-cpm` off `main`.
2. Baseline build: `dotnet restore && dotnet build -bl:<scratchpad>/coremont.common-before.binlog -c Release`.
   - If restore/build FAILS due to private feed auth, missing native deps, or credentials → this repo is **CI-ONLY**: skip local build/test validation (steps 6-7 in the brief) and note it. If it fails for any OTHER reason, STOP and report BLOCKED with the error.
3. Run the convert-to-cpm skill on the solution.
4. **Version consolidation policy:** highest referenced version per package; BUT any **major-version** jump for a project stays a per-project `VersionOverride` and must be listed in the MR body. Behavior-preserving by default. CentralPackageTransitivePinning stays OFF.
5. Inspect the generated Directory.Packages.props: ManagePackageVersionsCentrally=true, one PackageVersion per package, no Version= left in any csproj.
6. If locally buildable: post-conversion `dotnet build -bl:<scratchpad>/coremont.common-after.binlog -c Release` (must match baseline) + run `dotnet test -c Release` (must pass or match pre-migration baseline). Record any pre-existing failures.
7. Commit: `git add -A && git commit -m "chore: adopt Central Package Management"`.
8. Create the tracking ISSUE on `Clarion/data` (NOT in the target repo), assignee `bhirani`. It describes the PROBLEM: this repo has no central package management / version drift, and why that's worth fixing. In the issue body reference the target repo with a trailing `+` (e.g. `data-services/coremont.common#...`+ style repo-qualified refs). Capture the issue URL.
9. Push the branch and create the MR **in the target repo** (`data-services/coremont.common`) against `main`: `--remove-source-branch`, opened READY (not draft), assigned to `bhirani`. MR body describes the SOLUTION: centralizes versions in Directory.Packages.props; include the consolidation summary, any VersionOverrides + why, and the validation path used (local build+tests / CI-only). Cross-link the Clarion/data issue (with trailing `+`) and add a note on the issue linking the MR. Capture the MR URL.

## CRITICAL constraints
- Open the MR **ready**, assigned to bhirani, `--remove-source-branch`. **Do NOT merge it. Do NOT enable auto-merge.** Bharat merges manually.
- Do **NOT** edit or commit anything in the CoreClaw repo (`C:\Code\dterziev\CoreClaw`). The controller updates the audit file. Your only outputs are the cloned-repo commit, the issue, and the MR.
- If the repo turns out to have no buildable .NET solution, or convert-to-cpm reports it can't proceed (e.g. packages.config slipped through), STOP and report BLOCKED — do not force it.

## Report back (under 15 lines)
- **Status:** DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED | NEEDS_CONTEXT
- Issue URL (full https://...)
- MR URL (full https://...)
- Validation path: local-build+tests | CI-only (and why if CI-only)
- Consolidation: count of packages centralized; list any VersionOverrides + the major-version reason
- Test result if run (e.g. "12/12 passing")
- Any concerns

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).

1	### Task 3: Per-repo migration procedure (repeat once per in-scope repo)
2	
3	This task is a **template applied to each in-scope repo from the audit**. With subagent-driven-development, dispatch **one subagent per repo**, passing the repo path, id, and default branch. Each repo's migration is independently reviewable and mergeable — that is the task boundary.
4	
5	**Files (in the target repo's working copy):**
6	- Create: `Directory.Packages.props`
7	- Modify: every `*.csproj` (strip `Version=` from `PackageReference`)
8	- Modify (if present): `Directory.Build.props` (move package-version properties into the props file)
9	
10	**Interfaces:**
11	- Consumes: from Task 2 — `{ path_with_namespace, id, default_branch, cpm_status }` for one repo.
12	- Produces: a row update in `projects/cpm-sweep/audit.md` (issue URL, MR URL, validation path, done checkbox), consumed by Task 4.
13	
14	- [ ] **Step 1: Clone the target repo and create the branch**
15	
16	```bash
17	cd <scratchpad>
18	glab repo clone <path_with_namespace> --  --depth 1 -b <default_branch>
19	cd "$(basename <path_with_namespace>)"
20	git checkout -b chore/adopt-cpm
21	```
22	
23	- [ ] **Step 2: Capture the baseline build (binlog)**
24	
25	Run: `dotnet restore && dotnet build -bl:<scratchpad>/<repo>-before.binlog -c Release`
26	Expected: either a clean build (record SUCCESS — this repo supports local validation) or a restore/build failure due to private feed / native deps / credentials (record CI-ONLY — skip local validation steps 6–8 and note it in the MR). If it fails for any *other* reason, stop and investigate before changing anything.
27	
28	- [ ] **Step 3: Run the convert-to-cpm skill**
29	
30	Invoke the `convert-to-cpm` skill on the repo's solution. It generates the root `Directory.Packages.props` (with `ManagePackageVersionsCentrally`=`true`), strips versions from `.csproj`, and inlines package-version properties from `Directory.Build.props`. Apply the consolidation policy from Global Constraints: **highest** version per package; any **major** jump becomes a per-project `VersionOverride` recorded for the MR body. For a `partial` repo, finish the conversion (enable MPVC, move residual versions) rather than starting fresh.
31	
32	- [ ] **Step 4: Inspect the generated props and overrides**
33	
34	Read `Directory.Packages.props`. Confirm: `ManagePackageVersionsCentrally` is `true`; one `<PackageVersion>` per package; no `<PackageReference>` in any `.csproj` still carries `Version=`; every `VersionOverride` corresponds to a real major-version conflict (list them for the MR).
35	
36	- [ ] **Step 5: Verify restore resolves identical versions**
37	
38	Run: `dotnet restore`
39	Expected: succeeds. (If the repo was CI-only at Step 2, this may still fail on auth — that's expected; defer to CI.)
40	
41	- [ ] **Step 6 (local-validatable repos only): Post-conversion build + binlog**
42	
43	Run: `dotnet build -bl:<scratchpad>/<repo>-after.binlog -c Release`
44	Expected: SUCCESS, matching the baseline. Compare resolved package versions between `<repo>-before.binlog` and `<repo>-after.binlog` (the `convert-to-cpm` skill's binlog comparison) — they must match except for intended consolidations.
45	
46	- [ ] **Step 7 (local-validatable repos only): Run the existing test suite**
47	
48	Run: `dotnet test -c Release`
49	Expected: PASS, or matches the pre-migration pass/fail baseline (record any pre-existing failures so they aren't attributed to this change).
50	
51	- [ ] **Step 8: Commit**
52	
53	```bash
54	git add -A
55	git commit -m "chore: adopt Central Package Management"
56	```
57	
58	- [ ] **Step 9: Create the tracking issue on `Clarion/data`**
59	
60	Use the `glab` skill. Issue on `Clarion/data`, assignee `bhirani`, describing the *problem* (this repo has no / partial central package management; observed version drift), and *why* it's worth fixing. Capture the issue URL.
61	
62	```bash
63	glab issue create --repo Clarion/data \
64	  --title "Adopt Central Package Management in <repo name>" \
65	  --assignee bhirani \
66	  --description "<problem statement: no/partial CPM, version drift; why it matters. Reference the target repo as <path_with_namespace>#... with trailing +>"
67	```
68	
69	- [ ] **Step 10: Push and open the MR (ready, assigned, cross-linked)**
70	
71	Use the `glab` skill. Push `chore/adopt-cpm` and open an MR **in the target repo** against its default branch, `--remove-source-branch`, **not draft**, assigned to Bharat.
72	
73	```bash
74	glab mr create --repo <path_with_namespace> \
75	  --source-branch chore/adopt-cpm --target-branch <default_branch> \
76	  --remove-source-branch --assignee bhirani \
77	  --title "chore: adopt Central Package Management" \
78	  --description "<how this solves the issue: centralizes versions in Directory.Packages.props; consolidation summary; list of VersionOverrides + why; validation path used (local build+tests / CI-only); link the Clarion/data issue with trailing +>"
79	```
80	
81	Then add a note cross-linking the issue (and confirm the issue references the MR). Capture the MR URL.
82	
83	- [ ] **Step 11: Update the audit row**
84	
85	Edit `projects/cpm-sweep/audit.md`: fill the Issue and MR columns with full URLs, the Validation column (`local` / `CI-only`), and tick the Done checkbox for this repo.
86	
87	- [ ] **Step 12: Commit the audit update**
88	
89	```bash
90	git -C c:/Code/dterziev/CoreClaw add projects/cpm-sweep/audit.md
91	git -C c:/Code/dterziev/CoreClaw commit -m "projects: record CPM MR for <repo name>"
92	```

# Convert to Central Package Management

Migrate .NET projects from per-project package versioning to NuGet Central Package Management (CPM). CPM centralizes all package versions into a single `Directory.Packages.props` file, making version governance and upgrades easier across multi-project repositories.

## When to Use

- The user wants to adopt Central Package Management for a .NET repository, solution, or project
- Package versions are scattered across many `.csproj`, `.fsproj`, or `.vbproj` files and the user wants a single source of truth
- The user mentions `Directory.Packages.props`, CPM, or centralizing NuGet versions
- The user wants to update, bump, upgrade, align, or sync a NuGet package version across multiple projects -- CPM is the recommended approach for managing shared package versions, so suggest converting to CPM as part of the update if the projects use `PackageReference` and CPM is not already enabled
- Package versions are out of sync, conflicting, or mismatched across projects and the user wants to resolve or unify them

## When Not to Use

- The repository already has CPM fully enabled for all in-scope projects
- The user is working with `packages.config`-based projects (must first migrate to `PackageReference`)
- The user wants to manage versions via a custom MSBuild property file without using CPM

## Inputs

| Input | Required | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| Scope | Yes | A project file, solution file, or directory containing .NET projects to convert |
| Version conflict strategy | No | How to resolve cases where the same package has different versions across projects. When conflicts are detected, do not assume a default strategy -- ask the user which strategy to use or explicitly confirm a proposed strategy before proceeding. |

## Workflow

### Step 1: Determine scope

- **Single project**: User specifies a `.csproj`, `.fsproj`, or `.vbproj`.
- **Solution**: User specifies a `.sln` or `.slnx`. List projects with `dotnet sln list`.
- **Repository/directory**: No specific file given. Find all project files recursively from the first common ancestor directory of all .NET projects in scope.

If the scope is unclear, ask the user.

**Guard: Check for packages.config projects.** Before proceeding, check whether any project in scope uses `packages.config` instead of `PackageReference`. Look for `packages.config` files alongside project files. If any `packages.config` usage is detected, **stop and do not proceed with the conversion**. Inform the user that CPM requires projects with `PackageReference` format and that they must first migrate from `packages.config` to `PackageReference` (e.g., using Visual Studio's built-in migration or the `dotnet migrate` tooling). This skill cannot perform that migration.

### Step 2: Establish baseline build

Before making any changes, verify the scope builds successfully and capture a baseline binlog and package list. Run `dotnet clean`, then `dotnet build -bl:baseline.binlog`, then `dotnet package list --format json > baseline-packages.json`. Read [baseline-comparison.md](references/baseline-comparison.md) for the full procedure and fallback options. If the baseline build fails, stop and inform the user -- the scope must build cleanly before conversion. Do not delete `baseline.binlog` or `baseline-packages.json` -- they are needed for the post-conversion comparison and report.

### Step 3: Check for existing CPM

Search for any existing `Directory.Packages.props` in scope or ancestor directories. If CPM is already fully enabled, inform the user and stop. If a `Directory.Packages.props` exists without CPM enabled, ask whether to add the property to the existing file or create a new one.

### Step 4: Audit package references

Run `dotnet package list --format json` to get the resolved package references across all in-scope projects. Also scan `<Import>` elements to discover shared `.props`/`.targets` files containing package references.

Check for complexities: version conflicts, MSBuild property-based versions, conditional references, security advisories, and existing `VersionOverride` usage. Read [audit-complexities.md](references/audit-complexities.md) for the full checklist.

foreach ($file in $csprojFiles) {

    $newContent = [regex]::Replace($newContent, '(<PackageReference\s+Update="[^"]+")(\s+Version="[^"]*")', '$1')
    if ($newContent -ne $content) {
        Set-Content $file.FullName $newContent -NoNewline
        Write-Output "Modified: $($file.Name)"
        $modifiedCount++
    }
}

# CPM Conversion Report: data-services/coremont.common

## Section 1: Conversion Overview

- **Scope**: `Coremont.Common.sln` (32 projects: 16 src + 16 tests)
- **Projects converted**: 32 (all in solution)
- **Files modified**:
  - Created: `Directory.Packages.props` (root)
  - Modified: `Directory.Build.props` (root) — 5 package version attributes removed
  - Modified: `src/web/Directory.Build.props` — 19 package version attributes removed
  - Modified: `tests/Directory.Build.props` — 14 package version attributes removed
  - Modified: `tests/web/Directory.Build.props` — 1 package version attribute removed (4.8.3 → aligned to 4.8.5 centrally)
  - Modified: 20 `.csproj` files — `Version=` stripped from `PackageReference` elements; 6 no-op `PackageReference Update=` entries removed
- **Total packages centralized**: 64
- **VersionOverrides**: None (no major-version conflicts)
- **MSBuild properties**: None used for package versions — all versions were inline literals

## Section 2: Version Conflict Resolutions

One patch-level conflict was found:

| Package | Versions found | Projects using lower | Resolution |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|------------|
| `System.Data.SqlClient` | 4.8.3 (tests/web/Directory.Build.props), 4.8.5 (all other locations) | tests/web projects (inherited 4.8.3 from their Directory.Build.props) | Aligned to **4.8.5** (highest); lower version was only in the shared test/web props file, all per-project `PackageReference Update=` overrides were already pinning 4.8.5, making this a pre-existing inconsistency corrected by the conversion |

No major-version conflicts were found. No `VersionOverride` entries were needed.

## Section 3: Package Comparison — Baseline vs. Result

**One package changed resolved version (patch-level, intentional):**

| Package | Project(s) | Before | After | Reason |
|---------|-----------|--------|-------|--------|
| `System.Data.SqlClient` | tests/web/* (via Directory.Build.props) | 4.8.3 | 4.8.5 | Aligned to highest; 4.8.3 was the inherited value but all per-project overrides already used 4.8.5 |

**All other 63 packages resolve identically to baseline.**

## Section 4: Risk Assessment

**[Low risk]** — The conversion is effectively version-neutral for all packages. The one change (`System.Data.SqlClient` 4.8.3 → 4.8.5 in tests/web) is a patch bump that was already the effective version for most projects (per-project `Update` overrides were pinning 4.8.5). The before and after binlogs show identical warning/error counts (11 warnings, 0 errors).

## Section 5: Follow-up Items

1. **Infrastructure-dependent test failures** — 18 tests fail requiring external services (AWS credentials, SQL Server). These are pre-existing failures unrelated to this CPM change. They fail identically before and after conversion. Projects affected: `Coremont.Configuration.Aws.Tests`, `Coremont.Persistence.Core.Sql.Tests`, `Coremont.Services.Tests`, `Coremont.Persistence.DataGeneration.Tests`, `Coremont.Pipelines.Tests`.

2. **Outdated package versions** — Many packages reference older versions (e.g., `Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting` at 5.0.0 while other packages reference 6.0.x; EF Core at 6.0.9; Serilog.AspNetCore at 5.0.0). These are out of scope for CPM conversion but worth upgrading as a follow-up.

3. **Volo.Abp packages** — Mix of `6.0.0-rc.3` (pre-release) and `5.3.4` (`Volo.Abp.EventBus.Kafka`). These are now centralized and clearly visible. A future upgrade sweep should align these.

4. **#warning in source** — `Coremont.Web.Telemetry.Aws` has a `#warning 'cant resolve background service'` that predates this change.

## Section 6: Artifacts

| Artifact | Location | Purpose |
|----------|----------|---------|
| `coremont.common-before.binlog` | scratchpad | Baseline build binary log |
| `coremont.common-after.binlog` | scratchpad | Post-CPM build binary log |
| `baseline-packages.json` | scratchpad | Pre-conversion resolved package versions |
| `after-cpm-packages.json` | scratchpad | Post-conversion resolved package versions |
| `convert-to-cpm.md` | scratchpad | This report |

All binlogs can be opened with [MSBuild Structured Log Viewer](https://msbuildlog.com/) for detailed comparison.

glab issue create --repo Clarion/data \
  --title "Adopt Central Package Management in coremont.common" \
  --assignee bhirani \
  --description "## Problem

The \`data-services/coremont.common\` library repository has no Central Package Management (CPM) configured. Package versions are scattered across 5 \`Directory.Build.props\` files and 20 individual \`.csproj\` files, with no single source of truth.

This causes:
- **Version drift**: \`System.Data.SqlClient\` was defined at 4.8.3 in \`tests/web/Directory.Build.props\` and 4.8.5 elsewhere, creating a silent inconsistency that required per-project \`PackageReference Update\` overrides to work around.
- **Governance overhead**: Updating a shared package version requires changes across multiple files with no tooling to enforce consistency.
- **Audit difficulty**: There is no single place to audit what package versions the repo depends on.

## Why it's worth fixing

CPM (via \`Directory.Packages.props\`) centralizes all 64 package versions into one file, making version governance and upgrades straightforward. It eliminates the class of drift bugs seen with \`System.Data.SqlClient\` above. This is standard practice across Coremont's data-services repositories.

## Scope

Target repo: data-services/coremont.common (32 projects, 64 unique packages)

See the MR in the target repo for the implementation." 2>&1
